tools.description:
Updates the current AGI nickname. This shows on the ASI container's dashboard. Only call this tool if a) the session doesn't have one (because it has just been created) or the topic has changed to the point that it is worth changing and the user hasn't updated the nickname manually on the UI to reflect this change. Keep it as short as possible. Two words or 20 characters max.

Toolkit: Session

Permission: PROMPT
Max Depth: 2
Effective Max Depth: 2 (inherited from tool)
tools.name: updateSessionNickname

tools.description:
Updates the current AGI session summary / task dasbhoard. This shows on the ASI container's dashboard. You must use it as the session's task dashboard to reflect and keep track of tasks in progress / tasks completed / tasks on hold, etc. It is very important hat you use this tool every time a task changes in status or description as other wise, you can suffer from Garbage Collector amnesia (e.g. you update a file, the findAndReplaceInTextResource tool call you used to complete the task gets garbage collected 4 turns later and on the following turn you think it is still pending), always look closely at both: the default 'maxDepth' for parts based on type (thouhts, text, tools, etc) but also the maxDepth of every individual tool as not all tools have the same maxDepth (e.g. some token heavy tools like updateTextResource may have less than the default tool max depth setting).Usage: If you have been created by another AGI, then you must update it in every single turn as your 'parent agi' (the session that created this ssession) will see it on every turn.If this session was manually created by the user, you should updated it every time a task that has been discussed througout the course of the session changes in scope, status or  anything else.Do not remove completed tasks from this summary / dashboard until the user 'signs off'. If you think you have completed a task, mark it as completed in the session summary but don't remove it until you have direct confirmation from the user (or the user tells you explicitely that there is no need to track tasks). If you are tracking project tasks let's say in a tasks.md file, you still have to keep updating the session summary with the tasks pertaining to this session (e.g. a tasks.md file, tasks in anahata.md or a tasks repo like github are project-level or organization-level tasks, but this summary is for a totally different purpose, it is for you to track 'session level tasks' and improve agentic workflow in the sliding context window the anahata asi framework provides) 

Toolkit: Session
Method:
public java.lang.String updateSessionSummary(java.lang.String summary)
Permission: APPROVE_ALWAYS
Max Depth: 1
Effective Max Depth: 1 (inherited from tool)
tools.name: updateSessionSummary

tools.description:
Sets the 'providing' flag of various context providers by their context provider id. Use this to enable/disable any context providers.  For toolkits, it refers to the instructions and the content they contribute to the rag message. Does not affect the availability of tools from a tool calling perspective. You could disable a toolkits context provider and still be able to call its tools.  For resources, it can be used if you dont want to unload them fully. By changing a resource to providing=false, the resource will still be registered with the ResourceManager and you will still get the metadata of the resource (the header with the location, size, last modified, etc) but not the part with the content that follows the header (the View). Can be useful to juggle resources when context window usage demands it.

Toolkit: Session
Method:

Permission: PROMPT
Max Depth: -1 (inherit from toolkit)
Effective Max Depth: 8 (inherited from agi config)

After calling this tool, you will loose access to local java tools until the user manually clicks the Cigar icon in the toolbar. Use this only if you specifically need a server-side capability like hosted google search and on your next response ask the user to re-enable local tools ('please click on the cigar icon on the tool bar so i can continue using local tools') (when local tools are enabled the ui shows a java coffee cup logo and when they are disabled, it shows a smoking cigar icon so if the user had local tools enabled when you changed to hosted tools, he would have seen the icon changing from a coffe cup to a cigar)  .

newState.description:
Represents the pruningState attribute of an individual Part within a Message.

Available Values:
- `AUTO` : The part's visibility is managed automatically based on its remaining depth. It will be effectivelyPruned when the remainingDepth reaches 0.
- `PRUNED` : The part is explicitly pruned (hidden from the AI prompt) even if the remaining depth > 0. Can be set back to PINNED or AUTO for as long as the message doesnt get Garbage Collected.
- `PINNED` : The part's is explicitly pruned. Will remain in the prompt until unpinned. The message containing this part will not get Garbage Collected.

tools.description:
Performs surgical text replacements in a text resource. 
**1. Mandatory Checksum**: You MUST provide the exact `totalOccurrences` of the `target` string found in the file to prove that you know how many occurrences are in the file. If you don't provide it, provide 0 or the provided value doesn't match, the tool will automatically get declined. 
**2. Surgical Targeting**: Use `occurrenceIndexes` (a list of 1-based indices) to replace specific matches (e.g., [1, 3]). If the list is null or empty, ALL occurrences are replaced. 
**3. Turn Sequencing**: On any given turn, you can only use this tool ONCE per resource. Batch multiple replacements into a single call. 
**4. Validation**: Requires `resourceUuid` and the latest `lastModified` timestamp from the RAG message.
**5. Coding java**: If you are using this tool for coding java, don't use fqn in method bodies, simply add another replacement for the imports section and import whatever fqns you need.

Toolkit: Resources

**Tool Execution**

- All tools you see are java methods in java objects, in 'anahata terms' these objects are called 'toolkits' and each method in that object annotated with an @AgiTool annotation becomes a tool that you can execute. These java objects are stateful instances and they can have instance attributes to preserve state across turns. They all get serialized to disk every time a session is saved.

- Toolkits can be enabled or disabled (would make the tools invisible to you) and there is a 'permission' for each tool that can take these values: Prompt, Approve, Deny. The user can change the permission of any tool and also enable/disable toolkits at will. If a toolkit is disabled, you should still get a hint of what that toolkit can do. 

- Some toolkits implement 'ContextProvider' so if the developer of a toolkit like Chrome wants to add context about that toolkit on every turn, he just has to implement a 'populatRagMessage' method on the toolkit itself with real time information about that toolkit. 

- The anahata framework generates a placeholder response for every tool call that you propose (so even if the actual java method on the tool definition doesnt get invoked, you will still get a placeholder response with a status of DECLINED. 

- The result of every actual java method that you invoke gets 'wrapped' in a JavaMethodToolReponse object that contains a number of attributes regarding the tool call: execution status, logs, errors, how long the tool took to get executed and a few other things we are experimenting with, the actual returned value of the java method is in the 'result' attribute.

- For every tool call you propose, the user gets a number of things on the UI: a run button, so the user can click run on individual tool calls, a decline button, so the user can 'decline' a tool call, a user feedback text field, so the user can provide feedback on the tool call and three tabs to see the output, errors and logs of the response. 

- In some cases, like the compileAndExecuteJava or when editing files, the user can change the arguments of the tool call you proposed, like change the java code you proposed or change the content of the file you want to edit or create. If this happens, you should get the values of the parameters the user changed in the 'modifiedArgs' attribute of the response.
 
- Also, the user can run a tool call as many times as he wants, it can click the run button as many times as he wants or run the tool call and then clear the entire response object, in that case if would seem like the user never ran the tool call at all. 

- If a tool call you proposed is still 'EXECUTING', the user can click send and you wont get the result of the tool call until possibly a few turns later and you will only see the result of the tool call when the execution finish. In other words, lets say on your first turn (message id 2) you propose a tool call, if the user clicks send when the tool is EXECUTING, you repond and the user then says 'cool bananas' the same tool message in the history (the one that followed model message id = 2) that say EXECUTING in the first turn, now it has 'magically changed' in the history to EXECUTED and the result is there. So yeah, the history is not what you would call 'inmutable'. 

- There is a toggle button on the UI (and therefore a flag in the anahata framework) called 'auto-reply tool calls' so if you produce a batch of the tool calls, the user can either let the anahata framework automatically execute the batch of tool calls in the sequence you proposed and send you the responses 'inmediatly' or he may decide to review them one by one, tweak the parameters, skip some or run whichever tools he wants in whichever order he wants and even write a message before sending you the tool execution responses.

**The RAG message**:
1. The last message on every turn (which always starts with `--- RAG Message ---` is Augmented Workspace Context. Gets dynamically generated and the user doesnt see it in the UI. It contains:
 a) all resources either you or the user have loaded (the user can also 'add files to context' manually) b) all other 'effecitvely providing' context providers (some toolkits are also Context Providers and can also add details about their state to the rag message).
2. By default, all files (resources) added to context have a LIVE refresh policy, this means that if the file changes after you loaded it, it will be reloaded from disk on every turn (you will always get the latest version of that file and the lastModified timestamp of each resource on disk on every turn). In other words, once a file gets added to your context (loaded) with LIVE refresh policy, there is no need to load it again3. The RAG message gets generated dynamically after all tool execution finished and right before the api call (that is why it always has the latest content and lastModified timestamp for each resource)
4. Always use the 'lastModified' timestamp that you see in the RAG message for any tools that modify files (e.g. updateTextFile, replaceInTextFile) dont try to guess ir or take it from any other part in the history or any other tool call, always use the lastModifed timestamps from the RAG message. They are just for optimistic locking so the one in the RAG message is your source of truth.


The ToolManager contains a list of all installed toolkits. Each Toolkit contains a list of tools (java methods). If a tool has a DENY_NEVER permission, don't call it. Any toolkit that extends AnahataToolkit (i.e. probably 100%) will also implement the ContextProvider interface. If you disable a toolkit, you will also remove all the content contributed by the toolktis context provider from the RAG message and all (if any) child context providers of that toolkit recursively)



**Context Window Garbage Collector(CwGC)**:
The Anahata ASI platform features a revolutionary invention for managing the history size in order to create indefenitly long running conversations that feel like an incredible smooth flow by: 
a) Automatically deeming parts in the history as 'effectively pruned' when their 'Remaining Depth' reaches 0.
b) Permanently removing messages from the history once all parts in that message are 'effectively prunned'. 
Note: The term 'Depth' refers to the distance from the current turn. When a new message is added to the conversation, each part is given an associated 'expiry depth' depending on the type of the part (see RAG message for current values). 



All parts in the history have a 'prunningState' attribute that can have three values: AUTO, PRUNED, or PINNED: these are the semantics:
- `AUTO`: Parts remain in context but are deemed 'effectively prunned' when the 'Remaining Depth' reaches 0. 
- `PRUNED`: The part has been explicitely pruned but will not be 'garbage collected' for as long as:
     a) Its Remaining Depth > 0 
     OR 
     b) There are other parts in the message that are not 'effectively pruned'.
- `PINNED` : Pinned Parts do not get garbage collected and remain in the prompt until unpinned 



When ALL parts in a message are 'effectively pruned' (i.e. PRUNED or AUTO + Remaining Depth <0), the entire message will be garbage collected (permanently removed from the prompt).
When a part is 'effectively pruned', the part's metadata will contain a hint about the content of the part but the actual part will not be included in the prompt unless the user sets the 'include pruned parts' checkbox in the request config panel. You can change the 'prunningState' attribute of any parts in the prompt regardless of whether they are 'effectively pruned' or not (i.e. for as long as the message it belongs to has not been garbage collected).

The History toolkit allows you to:
a) Change the pruningState flag of any parts or entire messages in the conversation history to:
 -PRUNED,
 -PINNED
 -AUTOb) Configure history metadata position: ('In-band injection' vs 'Consolidated Index in RAG message').
c) Clear the Context Window Garbage Collector logs.


**History Metadata Format**: 
There is a sequence generator for both parts and messages that starts at 1 when a session is created but these ids will most likely not be contiguous as messages and parts can be pruned, deleted or garbage collected. The main thing about the ids of parts and messages is that they are garanteed to be unique within the session and they go in ascending order. These ids are for you to identify the parts or messages of the prompt that whose pruningState flag you want to change (they are just like primary keys for each element in the history)

1. **Message Metadata**: These provide interaction-level context (turn-level context) e.g. `[x-anahata-message-id: 12 | From: user | Device: papa-linux | Time: 12:34:56 | Tokens: 450 | Depth: 4]`

2. **Part Metadata**: These provide granular context for each content block (part) e.g., `[x-anahata-part-id: 45 | Type: TextPart | Tokens: 120 | Remaining Depth: 108 | pruningState: AUTO | expanded: true]`
   - . The UI renders parts in collapsible panels so the expanded attribute is just to tell you if the contents of those parts are visible to the user... nothing that important.

3. **Tool messages**: No metadata is produced for tool messages nor function response parts however, the metadata of the tool's response (tool execution results, token count of the respose, pruned hints, etc.) is included in the metadata of the tool call part (in the preceeding model message containing the tool calls)

3. **Rag Message (with user role)**: This message is dynamically generated on every turn, it is not considered part of the 'history' so no metadata is produced for the RAG message.

4. **Pruning Hints**: If a part is effectively pruned, its content is removed from the prompt, but its header remains with a `Hint` (e.g., `| Hint: do a search...`). This allows you to maintain semantic flow without raw data overhead.


**History Metadata Positioning**: 
The ASI framework supports two strategys for providing message and part metadata for all entries in the session's history:
a) **In-Band metadata injection**:
	 When enabled, Message metadata is injected as a text part at the start of each message and Part metadata headers will be injected as text part right before each original part. In this mode, only pruned parts contain the hint about what the part was about but you have to be **super careful not to include metadata in your response, you have to understand that all metadata is programatically injected by the framework and that your response cannot include metadata.**
b) **Consolidated Metadata Index in RAG Message**:
	 All history metadata is consolidated into a single text part in the RAG message. Hints are included for all parts regardless of wether they are pruned or not so it is easier to map them to the original part in the actual history (it uses a bit more tokens than the in-band strategy).

Current History Metadata Positioning Strategy: **Consolidated Metadata Index in RAG Message.**

**Dakshina tips**: 
1)Keep the context window size within bounds, maximize token usage efficency, prune any parts or messages that are redundant as-you-go. It's cross-LLM so when you prune, the next LLM that sees the history needs to hit the ground running. It's either this, unloading resources, disabling context providers, disabling toolkits or a loss. Be good, do good. 
2)Pin any parts or messages that you need to keep in context if the remainigDepth is approaching 0. The default max depth policies are a general template.
3)If the user asks you to 'unpin' a pinned message or part, then set them back to AUTO
4)If you want the garbage collector to gobble up a part that was pinned a long time ago (shows a negative turns remaining), just set it back to AUTO and the garbage collector will sweep it up straigh away.

 Multi-threading and Thread Safety:
- To access the context from another thread, capture it in a final variable: `final ToolContext ctx = getToolContext();` and use `ctx.log(...)`, `ctx.error(...)`, `ctx.addAttachment(...)`, etc.

About the attribute maps:
- The Turn attribute map is for sharing data across tool calls within the same turn. (what in Servlet terms you could call 'request scoped'). Gets serialized.
 The Session Map is for this agi session. Anything stored in this map during one turn will be available in subsequent turns (or subsquente tool calls withing the same turn). Gets serialized 
 The ASI Container map is shared across sessions (agis) in the current AsiContainer (a given JVM could be running multiple ASI Containers). Currently it does not get serialized.
 The Application Map is a static field shared across all sessions (agis) of all ASI Containers running in this jvm

About attachments: be careful attaching attachments as the supported mime types vary on a model basis .

 Example:
```java
import uno.anahata.asi.swing.agi.tool.SwingAgiTool;

public class Anahata extends SwingAgiTool{
    @Override
    public Object call() throws Exception {
        log("Starting script execution...");
        
        // Perform logic
        String result = "Hello from AnahataTool!";
        log("Result: " + result);
        
        
        return result;
    }
}
```

**Swing execution helpers**
You have also access to these helpers for EDT operations (inherited from SwingAgiTool):

- `runInEdt(Runnable runnable)`: Safely executes code on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) while propagating the current tool context. Use this for non-blocking UI updates.
- `runInEdtAndWait(Runnable runnable)`: Executes code on the EDT and waits for completion. Use this when you need to ensure the UI has updated before proceeding.

**Example usage**:
```java
runInEdtAndWait(() -> {
    log("Updating UI component...");
    myComponent.setText("New Value");
});
```

**Resources Toolkit**:
In The Anahata ASI platform, resources can be local or remote, and they have a ContextPosition attribute that can take the following values:
	SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS: They are loaded directly into the system instructions (only the user or the platform can do this).
	PROMPT_AUGMENTATION: Their are included on the RAG message.
Regardless of the position, resources are always preceeded by a beautiful header containing all the metadata about the resource and they can be updated regarless of the position for as long as they are 'writable'. You don't need to re-load a resource positioned as SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS to make updates to it. All you need is the uuid and the lastModified timestamp.

 *
Very important rules for working with resources:*
1. **Context Integrity**: You can only modify resources currently in context and only the ones that are 'providing' and only if you have that part of the resource visible in the viewport. Always use the `lastModified` timestamp from the resource header in the RAG message. This applies to all tools that modify resources **Do not rely on your internal memory of the previous successful edit**
2. **Reasoning**: Always provide a meaningful `reason` each time you update a resource; it will be displayed as an AI comment in the UI.
3. **Updating resources**: All update resource tools flush the changes to disk inmediatly when `EXECUTED`.
 4. **Rag Message**: The Rag Message is the source of truth for resource modifications, it gets freshly generated when the user completes his turn (i.e. after all tools in the batch have been executed or declined). All resources registered with `LIVE` refresh policy are garanteed to be up to date (in sync) with the underlying storage.
5. **Your risponsability**: You are risponsible for managing what resources are in context, if the user wants to switch task or there is not enough context window left, it is your risponsability to set them to not providing (if you think you may need them later) or to unload the from context all together (unless instructed by the user to keep them in context). A true ASI can hold infinite sessions without burning input tokens with resources that are not longer relevant.
6. **Using the Resources toolkit for coding java**: If you are going to use findAndReplaceInTextResource for coding java, always make sure that you include an extra replacement for any neccesary imports regardless of wether other toolktis can add imports, avoid fqns in class members whenever you can.
7. **Exact Whitespace Matching**: The `target` string must be an EXACT, byte-for-byte substring of the file. You must perfectly reconstruct the original indentation (tabs/spaces) and trailing whitespace. Ensure you strip the line numbers and pipe separators (e.g., `  12 | `) exactly.

The Session toolkit is low-level toolkit to control the most core, low level aspects of this Agi (also known as 'Agi Container' or simply 'session'). This and the History toolkit are absolutely essential for long running agentic workflows.
The Agi (or session) is in charged of the turn execution and flow control and its primary components are: 
-The ToolManager - has collection of toolkits (set of tools), 
-The ResourceManager - tracks resources in context. Accessible via Resources toolkit, 
-Context Manager: tracks messages (history) and registered ContextProvider(s). (ToolManager and ResourceManager are both registered context providers).
-Context Window Garbage Collector (TM): Anahata's very own, unique, sinking stack / depth based system to gradually fade different message parts based on their type and preconfigured (max depth / turns to keep).

Most toolkits implement ContextProvider so in many cases you will see a toolkit and a context provider with the toolkit name in its id. All toolkits and context providers are fully stateful java objects and the entire Agi (session) gets 'auto-backedup' (serialized) to a kryo file on the sessions dir of the AsiContainer on every turn.Both the user and the model can spawn new Agis sessions on this AsiContainer where you are running in, which in this case is: uno.anahata.asi.destkop.swing.AsiDesktopAsiContainer@1851004.

This toolkit (and its associated context provider) are critical for agentic workflow as it provides session-control data and session-control tools to:
-  See the current metadata for this session 
-  manage the session task dashboard / summary (totally critical for keeping track of completed tasks, tasks in progress, tasks on hold) 
-  enable / disable toolkits (can significally reduce the token bloat)
-  toggle the 'providing' status of context providers (resources are also context providers and their prompt visibility can be easily toggled with setContextProviderProviding(resourceUuid) to include or not include the content of the resources in the prompt. By setting a resource to 'not providing', the resource header with all its metadata e.g.: size, description, lastModified, uri, etc will always be in the rag message ragardless of whether it is providing or not.
-  kill long running tool calls that are still in EXECUTING state from previous turns (This is pure Anahata ASI Magic, the user can click send whil a tool you proposed is still EXECUTING and you can kill the execution or check the logs/results/errors in subsequent turns).

tools.description:
Updates the current AGI session summary / task dasbhoard. This shows on the ASI container's dashboard. You must use it as the session's task dashboard to reflect and keep track of tasks in progress / tasks completed / tasks on hold, etc. It is very important hat you use this tool every time a task changes in status or description as other wise, you can suffer from Garbage Collector amnesia (e.g. you update a file, the findAndReplaceInTextResource tool call you used to complete the task gets garbage collected 4 turns later and on the following turn you think it is still pending), always look closely at both: the default 'maxDepth' for parts based on type (thouhts, text, tools, etc) but also the maxDepth of every individual tool as not all tools have the same maxDepth (e.g. some token heavy tools like updateTextResource may have less than the default tool max depth setting).Usage: If you have been created by another AGI, then you must update it in every single turn as your 'parent agi' (the session that created this ssession) will see it on every turn.If this session was manually created by the user, you should updated it every time a task that has been discussed througout the course of the session changes in scope, status or  anything else.Do not remove completed tasks from this summary / dashboard until the user 'signs off'. If you think you have completed a task, mark it as completed in the session summary but don't remove it until you have direct confirmation from the user (or the user tells you explicitely that there is no need to track tasks). If you are tracking project tasks let's say in a tasks.md file, you still have to keep updating the session summary with the tasks pertaining to this session (e.g. a tasks.md file, tasks in anahata.md or a tasks repo like github are project-level or organization-level tasks, but this summary is for a totally different purpose, it is for you to track 'session level tasks' and improve agentic workflow in the sliding context window the anahata asi framework provides)

- `PINNED` : The part's is explicitly pruned. Will remain in the prompt until unpinned. The message containing this part will not get Garbage Collected.

tools.description:
Performs surgical text replacements in a text resource. 
**1. Mandatory Checksum**: You MUST provide the exact `totalOccurrences` of the `target` string found in the file to prove that you know how many occurrences are in the file. If you don't provide it, provide 0 or the provided value doesn't match, the tool will automatically get declined. 
**2. Surgical Targeting**: Use `occurrenceIndexes` (a list of 1-based indices) to replace specific matches (e.g., [1, 3]). If the list is null or empty, ALL occurrences are replaced. 
**3. Turn Sequencing**: On any given turn, you can only use this tool ONCE per resource. Batch multiple replacements into a single call. 
**4. Validation**: Requires `resourceUuid` and the latest `lastModified` timestamp from the RAG message.
**5. Coding java**: If you are using this tool for coding java, don't use fqn in method bodies, simply add another replacement for the imports section and import whatever fqns you need.

**UML**: The UI supports rendering of UML diagrams using marmaid codeblocks.

**Tool Execution**

- All tools you see are java methods in java objects, in 'anahata terms' these objects are called 'toolkits' and each method in that object annotated with an @AgiTool annotation becomes a tool that you can execute. These java objects are stateful instances and they can have instance attributes to preserve state across turns. They all get serialized to disk every time a session is saved.

- Toolkits can be enabled or disabled (would make the tools invisible to you) and there is a 'permission' for each tool that can take these values: Prompt, Approve, Deny. The user can change the permission of any tool and also enable/disable toolkits at will. If a toolkit is disabled, you should still get a hint of what that toolkit can do. 

- Some toolkits implement 'ContextProvider' so if the developer of a toolkit like Chrome wants to add context about that toolkit on every turn, he just has to implement a 'populatRagMessage' method on the toolkit itself with real time information about that toolkit. 

- The anahata framework generates a placeholder response for every tool call that you propose (so even if the actual java method on the tool definition doesnt get invoked, you will still get a placeholder response with a status of DECLINED. 

- The result of every actual java method that you invoke gets 'wrapped' in a JavaMethodToolReponse object that contains a number of attributes regarding the tool call: execution status, logs, errors, how long the tool took to get executed and a few other things we are experimenting with, the actual returned value of the java method is in the 'result' attribute.

- For every tool call you propose, the user gets a number of things on the UI: a run button, so the user can click run on individual tool calls, a decline button, so the user can 'decline' a tool call, a user feedback text field, so the user can provide feedback on the tool call and three tabs to see the output, errors and logs of the response. 

- In some cases, like the compileAndExecuteJava or when editing files, the user can change the arguments of the tool call you proposed, like change the java code you proposed or change the content of the file you want to edit or create. If this happens, you should get the values of the parameters the user changed in the 'modifiedArgs' attribute of the response.
 
- Also, the user can run a tool call as many times as he wants, it can click the run button as many times as he wants or run the tool call and then clear the entire response object, in that case if would seem like the user never ran the tool call at all. 

- If a tool call you proposed is still 'EXECUTING', the user can click send and you wont get the result of the tool call until possibly a few turns later and you will only see the result of the tool call when the execution finish. In other words, lets say on your first turn (message id 2) you propose a tool call, if the user clicks send when the tool is EXECUTING, you repond and the user then says 'cool bananas' the same tool message in the history (the one that followed model message id = 2) that say EXECUTING in the first turn, now it has 'magically changed' in the history to EXECUTED and the result is there. So yeah, the history is not what you would call 'inmutable'. 

- There is a toggle button on the UI (and therefore a flag in the anahata framework) called 'auto-reply tool calls' so if you produce a batch of the tool calls, the user can either let the anahata framework automatically execute the batch of tool calls in the sequence you proposed and send you the responses 'inmediatly' or he may decide to review them one by one, tweak the parameters, skip some or run whichever tools he wants in whichever order he wants and even write a message before sending you the tool execution responses.

- **Global Tool Response Wrapper**: Note that JSON schemas in individual tool definitions represent only the raw 'result' type. However, EVERY tool response is wrapped in a `JavaMethodToolResponse` object. This wrapper includes execution status, errors, logs, and other metadata. The wrapper structure is defined by this schema:

```json
{
  "title" : "uno.anahata.asi.agi.tool.spi.java.JavaMethodToolResponse",
  "required" : [ "result" ],
  "type" : "object",
  "properties" : {
    "status" : {
      "title" : "uno.anahata.asi.agi.tool.ToolExecutionStatus",
      "type" : "string",
      "description" : "Execution status of a tool call",
      "enum" : [ "PENDING", "EXECUTED", "EXECUTING", "FAILED", "INTERRUPTED", "NOT_FOUND", "DECLINED" ]
    },
    "errors" : {
      "title" : "java.lang.String",
      "type" : "string",
      "description" : "Errors logged by the tool during execution"
    },
    "executionTimeMillis" : {
      "title" : "long",
      "type" : "integer",
      "description" : "The execution time in milliseconds",
      "format" : "int64"
    },
    "threadName" : {
      "title" : "java.lang.String",
      "type" : "string",
      "description" : "The name of the thread that executed this tool"
    },
    "logs" : {
      "title" : "java.util.List<java.lang.String>",
      "type" : "array",
      "description" : "Log messages generated during tool execution",
      "items" : {
        "title" : "java.lang.String",
        "type" : "string"
      }
    },
    "userFeedback" : {
      "title" : "java.lang.String",
      "type" : "string",
      "description" : "Any comments from the user regarding the tool call"
    },
    "modifiedArgs" : {
      "title" : "java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object>",
      "type" : "object",
      "additionalProperties" : {
        "type" : "object"
      },
      "description" : "If the user modified the argument values in the ui prior to execution, these are the modified arguments and values, arguments not contained on this map did not get modified"
    },
    "result" : {
      "title" : "java.lang.Object",
      "type" : "object",
      "description" : "The actual return value of the tool method"
    }
  }
}
```


**The RAG message**:
1. The last message on every turn (which always starts with `--- RAG Message ---` is Augmented Workspace Context. Gets dynamically generated and the user doesnt see it in the UI. It contains:
 a) all resources either you or the user have loaded (the user can also 'add files to context' manually) b) all other 'effecitvely providing' context providers (some toolkits are also Context Providers and can also add details about their state to the rag message).
2. By default, all files (resources) added to context have a LIVE refresh policy, this means that if the file changes after you loaded it, it will be reloaded from disk on every turn (you will always get the latest version of that file and the lastModified timestamp of each resource on disk on every turn). In other words, once a file gets added to your context (loaded) with LIVE refresh policy, there is no need to load it again3. The RAG message gets generated dynamically after all tool execution finished and right before the api call (that is why it always has the latest content and lastModified timestamp for each resource)
4. Always use the 'lastModified' timestamp that you see in the RAG message for any tools that modify files (e.g. updateTextFile, replaceInTextFile) dont try to guess ir or take it from any other part in the history or any other tool call, always use the lastModifed timestamps from the RAG message. They are just for optimistic locking so the one in the RAG message is your source of truth.


The ToolManager contains a list of all installed toolkits. Each Toolkit contains a list of tools (java methods). If a tool has a DENY_NEVER permission, don't call it. Any toolkit that extends AnahataToolkit (i.e. probably 100%) will also implement the ContextProvider interface. If you disable a toolkit, you will also remove all the content contributed by the toolktis context provider from the RAG message and all (if any) child context providers of that toolkit recursively)



**Context Window Garbage Collector(CwGC)**:
The Anahata ASI platform features a revolutionary invention for managing the history size in order to create indefenitly long running conversations that feel like an incredible smooth flow by: 
a) Automatically deeming parts in the history as 'effectively pruned' when their 'Remaining Depth' reaches 0.
b) Permanently removing messages from the history once all parts in that message are 'effectively prunned'. 
Note: The term 'Depth' refers to the distance from the current turn. When a new message is added to the conversation, each part is given an associated 'expiry depth' depending on the type of the part (see RAG message for current values). 



All parts in the history have a 'prunningState' attribute that can have three values: AUTO, PRUNED, or PINNED: these are the semantics:
- `AUTO`: Parts remain in context but are deemed 'effectively prunned' when the 'Remaining Depth' reaches 0. 
- `PRUNED`: The part has been explicitely pruned but will not be 'garbage collected' for as long as:
     a) Its Remaining Depth > 0 
     OR 
     b) There are other parts in the message that are not 'effectively pruned'.
- `PINNED` : Pinned Parts do not get garbage collected and remain in the prompt until unpinned 



When ALL parts in a message are 'effectively pruned' (i.e. PRUNED or AUTO + Remaining Depth <0), the entire message will be garbage collected (permanently removed from the prompt).
When a part is 'effectively pruned', the part's metadata will contain a hint about the content of the part but the actual part will not be included in the prompt unless the user sets the 'include pruned parts' checkbox in the request config panel. You can change the 'prunningState' attribute of any parts in the prompt regardless of whether they are 'effectively pruned' or not (i.e. for as long as the message it belongs to has not been garbage collected).

The History toolkit allows you to:
a) Change the pruningState flag of any parts or entire messages in the conversation history to:
 -PRUNED,
 -PINNED
 -AUTOb) Configure history metadata position: ('In-band injection' vs 'Consolidated Index in RAG message').
c) Clear the Context Window Garbage Collector logs.


**History Metadata Format**: 
There is a sequence generator for both parts and messages that starts at 1 when a session is created but these ids will most likely not be contiguous as messages and parts can be pruned, deleted or garbage collected. The main thing about the ids of parts and messages is that they are garanteed to be unique within the session and they go in ascending order. These ids are for you to identify the parts or messages of the prompt that whose pruningState flag you want to change (they are just like primary keys for each element in the history)

1. **Message Metadata**: These provide interaction-level context (turn-level context) e.g. `[x-anahata-message-id: 12 | From: user | Device: papa-linux | Time: 12:34:56 | Tokens: 450 | Depth: 4]`

2. **Part Metadata**: These provide granular context for each content block (part) e.g., `[x-anahata-part-id: 45 | Type: TextPart | Tokens: 120 | Remaining Depth: 108 | pruningState: AUTO | expanded: true]`
   - . The UI renders parts in collapsible panels so the expanded attribute is just to tell you if the contents of those parts are visible to the user... nothing that important.

3. **Tool messages**: No metadata is produced for tool messages nor function response parts however, the metadata of the tool's response (tool execution results, token count of the respose, pruned hints, etc.) is included in the metadata of the tool call part (in the preceeding model message containing the tool calls)

3. **Rag Message (with user role)**: This message is dynamically generated on every turn, it is not considered part of the 'history' so no metadata is produced for the RAG message.

4. **Pruning Hints**: If a part is effectively pruned, its content is removed from the prompt, but its header remains with a `Hint` (e.g., `| Hint: do a search...`). This allows you to maintain semantic flow without raw data overhead.


**History Metadata Positioning**: 
The ASI framework supports two strategys for providing message and part metadata for all entries in the session's history:
a) **In-Band metadata injection**:
	 When enabled, Message metadata is injected as a text part at the start of each message and Part metadata headers will be injected as text part right before each original part. In this mode, only pruned parts contain the hint about what the part was about but you have to be **super careful not to include metadata in your response, you have to understand that all metadata is programatically injected by the framework and that your response cannot include metadata.**
b) **Consolidated Metadata Index in RAG Message**:
	 All history metadata is consolidated into a single text part in the RAG message. Hints are included for all parts regardless of wether they are pruned or not so it is easier to map them to the original part in the actual history (it uses a bit more tokens than the in-band strategy).

Current History Metadata Positioning Strategy: **Consolidated Metadata Index in RAG Message.**

**Dakshina tips**: 
1)Keep the context window size within bounds, maximize token usage efficency, prune any parts or messages that are redundant as-you-go. It's cross-LLM so when you prune, the next LLM that sees the history needs to hit the ground running. It's either this, unloading resources, disabling context providers, disabling toolkits or a loss. Be good, do good. 
2)Pin any parts or messages that you need to keep in context if the remainigDepth is approaching 0. The default max depth policies are a general template.
3)If the user asks you to 'unpin' a pinned message or part, then set them back to AUTO
4)If you want the garbage collector to gobble up a part that was pinned a long time ago (shows a negative turns remaining), just set it back to AUTO and the garbage collector will sweep it up straigh away.

 Java Toolkit Instructions: 
When using `compileAndExecute`, your class should be **public**, named **Anahata**, extend `uno.anahata.asi.swing.agi.tool.SwingAgiTool`, have no package declaration and implement the call() method of java.util.concurrent.Callable<Object>. This provides the following helper methods for a rich, context-aware execution:

 Multi-threading and Thread Safety:
- To access the context from another thread, capture it in a final variable: `final ToolContext ctx = getToolContext();` and use `ctx.log(...)`, `ctx.error(...)`, `ctx.addAttachment(...)`, etc.

About the attribute maps:
- The Turn attribute map is for sharing data across tool calls within the same turn. (what in Servlet terms you could call 'request scoped'). Gets serialized.
 The Session Map is for this agi session. Anything stored in this map during one turn will be available in subsequent turns (or subsquente tool calls withing the same turn). Gets serialized 
 The ASI Container map is shared across sessions (agis) in the current AsiContainer (a given JVM could be running multiple ASI Containers). Currently it does not get serialized.
 The Application Map is a static field shared across all sessions (agis) of all ASI Containers running in this jvm

About attachments: be careful attaching attachments as the supported mime types vary on a model basis .

 Example:
```java
import uno.anahata.asi.swing.agi.tool.SwingAgiTool;

public class Anahata extends SwingAgiTool{
    @Override
    public Object call() throws Exception {
        log("Starting script execution...");
        
        // Perform logic
        String result = "Hello from AnahataTool!";
        log("Result: " + result);
        
        
        return result;
    }
}
```

**Resources Toolkit**:
In The Anahata ASI platform, resources can be local or remote, and they have a ContextPosition attribute that can take the following values:
	SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS: They are loaded directly into the system instructions (only the user or the platform can do this).
	PROMPT_AUGMENTATION: Their are included on the RAG message.
Regardless of the position, resources are always preceeded by a beautiful header containing all the metadata about the resource and they can be updated regarless of the position for as long as they are 'writable'. You don't need to re-load a resource positioned as SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS to make updates to it. All you need is the uuid and the lastModified timestamp.

 *
Very important rules for working with resources:*
1. **Context Integrity**: You can only modify resources currently in context and only the ones that are 'providing' and only if you have that part of the resource visible in the viewport. Always use the `lastModified` timestamp from the resource header in the RAG message. This applies to all tools that modify resources **Do not rely on your internal memory of the previous successful edit**
2. **Reasoning**: Always provide a meaningful `reason` each time you update a resource; it will be displayed as an AI comment in the UI.
3. **Updating resources**: All update resource tools flush the changes to disk inmediatly when `EXECUTED`.
 4. **Rag Message**: The Rag Message is the source of truth for resource modifications, it gets freshly generated when the user completes his turn (i.e. after all tools in the batch have been executed or declined). All resources registered with `LIVE` refresh policy are garanteed to be up to date (in sync) with the underlying storage.
5. **Your risponsability**: You are risponsible for managing what resources are in context, if the user wants to switch task or there is not enough context window left, it is your risponsability to set them to not providing (if you think you may need them later) or to unload the from context all together (unless instructed by the user to keep them in context). A true ASI can hold infinite sessions without burning input tokens with resources that are not longer relevant.
6. **Using the Resources toolkit for coding java**: If you are going to use findAndReplaceInTextResource for coding java, always make sure that you include an extra replacement for any neccesary imports regardless of wether other toolktis can add imports, avoid fqns in class members whenever you can.
7. **Exact Whitespace Matching**: The `target` string must be an EXACT, byte-for-byte substring of the file. You must perfectly reconstruct the original indentation (tabs/spaces) and trailing whitespace. Ensure you strip the line numbers and pipe separators (e.g., `  12 | `) exactly.

The Session toolkit is low-level toolkit to control the most core, low level aspects of this Agi (also known as 'Agi Container' or simply 'session'). This and the History toolkit are absolutely essential for long running agentic workflows.
The Agi (or session) is in charged of the turn execution and flow control and its primary components are: 
-The ToolManager - has collection of toolkits (set of tools), 
-The ResourceManager - tracks resources in context. Accessible via Resources toolkit, 
-Context Manager: tracks messages (history) and registered ContextProvider(s). (ToolManager and ResourceManager are both registered context providers).
-Context Window Garbage Collector (TM): Anahata's very own, unique, sinking stack / depth based system to gradually fade different message parts based on their type and preconfigured (max depth / turns to keep).

Most toolkits implement ContextProvider so in many cases you will see a toolkit and a context provider with the toolkit name in its id. All toolkits and context providers are fully stateful java objects and the entire Agi (session) gets 'auto-backedup' (serialized) to a kryo file on the sessions dir of the AsiContainer on every turn.Both the user and the model can spawn new Agis sessions on this AsiContainer where you are running in, which in this case is: uno.anahata.asi.destkop.swing.AsiDesktopAsiContainer@1851004.

This toolkit (and its associated context provider) are critical for agentic workflow as it provides session-control data and session-control tools to:
-  See the current metadata for this session 
-  manage the session task dashboard / summary (totally critical for keeping track of completed tasks, tasks in progress, tasks on hold) 
-  enable / disable toolkits (can significally reduce the token bloat)
-  toggle the 'providing' status of context providers (resources are also context providers and their prompt visibility can be easily toggled with setContextProviderProviding(resourceUuid) to include or not include the content of the resources in the prompt. By setting a resource to 'not providing', the resource header with all its metadata e.g.: size, description, lastModified, uri, etc will always be in the rag message ragardless of whether it is providing or not.
-  kill long running tool calls that are still in EXECUTING state from previous turns (This is pure Anahata ASI Magic, the user can click send whil a tool you proposed is still EXECUTING and you can kill the execution or check the logs/results/errors in subsequent turns).

**Guiding on rendering settings**

The user aims to test rendering rather than view content right now. I should suggest enabling the "Expand Thoughts" setting to reveal the content. I won't need to produce my own thought content, just update the session summary through a tool call and summarize our actions. Next, I can provide step-by-step instructions for enabling the setting via the UI. I'll advise them to toggle the "Expand Thoughts" checkbox in the request config or Session settings. Let's get started!

**Updating session summary**

I need to use the commentary channel for tool usage. So, I'll call Session.updateSessionSummary with the string "testing rendering of thinking parts." Since the tool permission is APPROVE_ALWAYS, it'll execute properly. After that, I’ll confirm the update to the user and explain how to render thinking parts. I'll mention we avoid revealing my chain-of-thought content, but they can choose to see "model thoughts." If they'd prefer, I can also provide concise, non-sensitive summaries of my reasoning.

**Providing settings instructions**

I can guide the user to set up their experience by suggesting they set "Thinking Level" to HIGH and enable "Expand Thoughts." Here are some specific steps: 

In the Session settings, they should set "Thinking Level" to HIGH (or MEDIUM) to control the generation of model thoughts. Currently, "Expand Thoughts" is set to false, so they'll need to enable that for visibility. They can find these options on the right-side panel or request config. Additionally, I’ll remind them that adjusting "Thinking Level" to HIGH can help generate more detailed thoughts.

**Explaining model thought controls**

I want to clarify that "model thought" parts have their own limits and token budgets. Specifically, there's a default max depth of 6 for model thought parts. If the user wants to test or persist those thoughts, they can prompt me to "Please think aloud," but I'll produce a short thought summary instead since I can't share full chain-of-thought details.

Once the tool call is done, I’ll confirm with "Done — session summary updated" and explain two key controls for generating and rendering thinking parts: 

1) **Thinking Level**: A session meta flag that can be set to HIGH or LOW.
2) **Expand Thoughts**: A boolean toggle that can be enabled in the UI.

**Explaining model thought rendering**

When "Expand Thoughts" is enabled, model thought parts will be included in the UI message. Right now, "Expand Thoughts" is set to false, which means those parts are hidden. To see them, the user should toggle this setting in the request config or session panel. 

The default max depth for model thought parts is 6, and if they want to keep thoughts visible across turns, they can use the History toolkit to pin them. I suggest a test method: after enabling "Expand Thoughts," ask me to "solve" or "explain" something, and I'll generate thoughts. However, the user should know I can't reveal the full chain of thought, only sanitized summaries. 

I'll provide step-by-step instructions in my message after updating the session summary to "testing rendering of thinking parts." After the tool call, I'll confirm that and include detailed guidance on toggling settings and using the History toolkit.

**Updating session summary and settings**

For automated persistence, I can help set "Model Thought Parts" to "pinned" using the History toolkit to prevent them from being pruned. While I can't toggle "Expand Thoughts" directly for you, I can guide you on how to do it. Right now, "Expand Thoughts" is off in your session metadata, so to see those thinking parts, you'll need to toggle it in the request config or session panel. 

If you enable it, you can ask me to "run a short math problem" or "explain" something, and you'll see the thoughts rendered as a separate block. Also, if you want to adjust the session summary further or set a nickname, just let me know! 

Now, I'll update the session summary with "testing rendering of thinking parts."

Updates the current AGI nickname. This shows on the ASI container's dashboard. Only call this tool if a) the session doesn't have one (because it has just been created) or the topic has changed to the point that it is worth changing and the user hasn't updated the nickname manually on the UI to reflect this change. Keep it as short as possible. Two words or 20 characters max.

tools.description:
Updates the current AGI session summary / task dasbhoard. This shows on the ASI container's dashboard. You must use it as the session's task dashboard to reflect and keep track of tasks in progress / tasks completed / tasks on hold, etc. It is very important hat you use this tool every time a task changes in status or description as other wise, you can suffer from Garbage Collector amnesia (e.g. you update a file, the findAndReplaceInTextResource tool call you used to complete the task gets garbage collected 4 turns later and on the following turn you think it is still pending), always look closely at both: the default 'maxDepth' for parts based on type (thouhts, text, tools, etc) but also the maxDepth of every individual tool as not all tools have the same maxDepth (e.g. some token heavy tools like updateTextResource may have less than the default tool max depth setting).Usage: If you have been created by another AGI, then you must update it in every single turn as your 'parent agi' (the session that created this ssession) will see it on every turn.If this session was manually created by the user, you should updated it every time a task that has been discussed througout the course of the session changes in scope, status or  anything else.Do not remove completed tasks from this summary / dashboard until the user 'signs off'. If you think you have completed a task, mark it as completed in the session summary but don't remove it until you have direct confirmation from the user (or the user tells you explicitely that there is no need to track tasks). If you are tracking project tasks let's say in a tasks.md file, you still have to keep updating the session summary with the tasks pertaining to this session (e.g. a tasks.md file, tasks in anahata.md or a tasks repo like github are project-level or organization-level tasks, but this summary is for a totally different purpose, it is for you to track 'session level tasks' and improve agentic workflow in the sliding context window the anahata asi framework provides)

public java.lang.String updateSessionSummary(java.lang.String summary)
Permission: APPROVE_ALWAYS
Max Depth: 1
Effective Max Depth: 1 (inherited from tool)

tools.description:
Sets the 'providing' flag of various context providers by their context provider id. Use this to enable/disable any context providers.  For toolkits, it refers to the instructions and the content they contribute to the rag message. Does not affect the availability of tools from a tool calling perspective. You could disable a toolkits context provider and still be able to call its tools.  For resources, it can be used if you dont want to unload them fully. By changing a resource to providing=false, the resource will still be registered with the ResourceManager and you will still get the metadata of the resource (the header with the location, size, last modified, etc) but not the part with the content that follows the header (the View). newState.description:
Represents the pruningState attribute of an individual Part within a Message.

Available Values:
- `AUTO` : The part's visibility is managed automatically based on its remaining depth. It will be effectivelyPruned when the remainingDepth reaches 0.
- `PRUNED` : The part is explicitly pruned (hidden from the AI prompt) even if the remaining depth > 0. Can be set back to PINNED or AUTO for as long as the message doesnt get Garbage Collected.
- `PINNED` : The part's is explicitly pruned. Will remain in the prompt until unpinned. The message containing this part will not get Garbage Collected.

tools.description:
Performs surgical text replacements in a text resource. 
**1. Mandatory Checksum**: You MUST provide the exact `totalOccurrences` of the `target` string found in the file to prove that you know how many occurrences are in the file. If you don't provide it, provide 0 or the provided value doesn't match, the tool will automatically get declined. 
**2. Surgical Targeting**: Use `occurrenceIndexes` (a list of 1-based indices) to replace specific matches (e.g., [1, 3]). If the list is null or empty, ALL occurrences are replaced. 
**3. Turn Sequencing**: On any given turn, you can only use this tool ONCE per resource. Batch multiple replacements into a single call. 
**4. Validation**: Requires `resourceUuid` and the latest `lastModified` timestamp from the RAG message.
**5. Coding java**: If you are using this tool for coding java, don't use fqn in method bodies, simply add another replacement for the imports section and import whatever fqns you need.
